evidence.text: United Shoe was accused of deterring competition by allowing firms to use United Shoe machines...

editableNewDialogue.text: In antitrust, is it valid to argue that if two contract terms are each lawful on their own, then their combination must also be lawful?

Not necessarily. Antitrust often evaluates the *overall effect* of conduct in context, so two individually common terms can still be unlawful in combination if they materially foreclose rivals, raise switching costs, or help maintain monopoly power. The legality typically turns on market power, foreclosure share, duration, practical ability to multihome/switch, and any procompetitive justifications.
So what would you look at to decide whether the bundle is exclusionary rather than just efficient contracting?

Key screens include: (1) market power and entry barriers; (2) the percentage of demand foreclosed to rivals; (3) the “stickiness” created by long duration, penalties, or capacity/requirements provisions; (4) whether customers realistically can substitute or terminate; (5) evidence of harm (higher prices, reduced output/quality/innovation) versus plausible efficiencies (better service, financing, quality assurance); and (6) whether a less restrictive alternative could achieve the same efficiencies.
Give a simple hypothetical where the combination is the problem.

Suppose a dominant equipment supplier offers (a) a standard long-term lease and (b) a “full-capacity” requirement. Each term might be defensible alone (e.g., leases finance durable goods; capacity clauses ensure maintenance economies). But together they can effectively lock up customers’ entire demand for years, leaving rivals with too little scale to compete—especially if most major buyers sign, termination is costly, and entrants need minimum scale to survive.
Does antitrust treat this differently under per se vs rule of reason?

Yes. These sorts of contract bundles are usually analyzed under the rule of reason (or a structured effects-based framework), not per se illegality. That means the analysis weighs competitive harms against efficiencies, and the outcome depends heavily on facts like market shares, foreclosure, and business rationale.

Unrelated example often used in classes: in the 1912 Olympic pentathlon, the event mix (fencing, swimming, riding, shooting, running) was designed to test “all-around” capability—each event alone is straightforward, but the combined package changes what success means. Similarly, combining contract terms can change competitive effects.

On the United Shoe thing—what bugs me is how the full-capacity clause plus the long-term lease basically locks a shop into one supplier, especially when the contract runs right through the period when the machines would normally be replaced.

Right—each term in isolation can look innocuous, but together they can function like de facto exclusive dealing. If the lease duration lines up with the replacement cycle, the lessee never really gets a meaningful chance to switch, and the full-capacity requirement can foreclose using rivals for incremental demand. That combination can raise anticompetitive foreclosure concerns even if neither clause is automatically unlawful alone.
About that earlier prompt: “Eina and I are arguing about where to take our honeymoon…”—that wasn’t my real life. It was just a made-up scenario in the worksheet.
Got it—so “Eina” was just a hypothetical name in the example, not your actual partner.
Exactly. I don’t have a partner named Eina.
